_meta.purpose: Machine-readable memory log of clinical-research analytic patterns. Modeled on BioMedAgent's Redis memory cache. Read at the start of every session; append after every session that surfaces a new pattern.
_meta.maintainer: clinical-research-assistant skill, sourced from real sessions
_meta.usage: Scan trigger_patterns at session start. If any current-task pattern matches, apply the action directly without re-deriving. Append new entries with date + originating_session + trigger_patterns + lesson + action. Mark superseded entries deprecated rather than deleting.

lessons.originating_session: V3 Esophageal Cancer Disparity Analysis (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona)
trigger_patterns: any pp-gap reported on a continuous variable
lessons.lesson: An overall continuous-variable gap can be a stage-distribution (or other confounder-distribution) artefact rather than a within-stratum finding.
lessons.action: Whenever a continuous-variable pp/percent gap is reported, run within-stratum analysis on the most-relevant confounder (stage, age, histology) BEFORE publishing. If the gap disappears within strata, report it as a confounding artefact, not a finding.

lessons.lesson: Cox HR on the all-stages cohort can understate the disparity in the surgically-curable Stage I-III subset because Stage IV dilutes any access-mediated effect.
lessons.action: Report HR for both the all-stages cohort AND the mechanism-relevant subset (Stage I-III). State which is primary in the abstract.

trigger_patterns: surgical access acts in first 6 months
lessons.lesson: Schoenfeld test for race PH may be globally satisfied while time-stratified Cox reveals biphasic disparity.
lessons.action: When the research question implicates a time-bound mechanism, include time-stratified Cox in 0-6, 6-12, 12-24, and 24+ month windows even when global PH holds. Report HR per window with 95% CI.

lessons.lesson: Excluding patients with Unknown values for covariates can shift adjusted ORs materially when Unknown rate exceeds 5% (e.g., OR shifted from 0.59 complete-case to 0.75 inclusive).
lessons.action: When >5% of any covariate is Unknown, present BOTH inclusive (Unknown-as-category) and exclusive (complete-case) results. Add multiple imputation as a third sensitivity. Report all three side-by-side.

trigger_patterns: primary OR or HR in abstract
lessons.lesson: E-value (VanderWeele-Ding) quantifies the magnitude of unmeasured confounding required to explain a residual association. E-value of 2.78 (surgery OR 0.58) is strong; E-value of 1.57 (Cox HR 1.15) is modest.
lessons.action: Mandatory: report E-value (point estimate + CI bound) for every fully-adjusted residual exposure effect in the abstract and main results table. Use VanderWeele-Ding formula.

trigger_patterns: more than 5 hypothesis tests in same domain
lessons.lesson: Reporting >5 separate tests without family-wise correction risks false positives. BH-FDR can flip primary-significant findings to null after adjustment.
lessons.action: For papers with >5 hypothesis tests in the same domain, generate a master significance table at the end of analysis: Endpoint, Cohort, Adjusted estimate (95% CI), Primary P, BH-FDR Q, Bonferroni-significant Yes/No.

trigger_patterns: Hispanic or NHAPI minor-category cell counts
lessons.lesson: NCDB Data Use Agreement prohibits publication of any cell with N<11. Internal worksheets should keep raw counts; publication-facing exports must mask cells with N<11 as '<11'.
lessons.action: For any NCDB analysis, generate both a raw internal version and a DUA-compliant publication version with cells N<11 masked as '<11'. Include the DUA-compliant version as a separate Excel sheet labelled 'PUBLICATION_READY'.

lessons.lesson: Reporting binary 'late-stage' rates can obscure the actual disparity, which often lives in Stage I underrepresentation and Unknown-stage over-representation rather than Stage III/IV parity.
lessons.action: Decompose stage distribution into Stage I vs II vs III vs IV vs Unknown rather than binary late-stage. Treat Unknown-stage rate as its own outcome (workup-completeness disparity).

trigger_patterns: post-CheckMate-577 cohort with limited NHB n
lessons.lesson: Subgroup analyses with one arm <500 can yield non-significant findings simply due to insufficient power. The point estimate may be in the same direction as the primary but underpowered to detect.
lessons.action: For any subgroup analysis with one arm <500, compute power for the observed effect size before interpreting. Report findings honestly as 'trend, not significant; likely underpowered (minimum detectable gap X pp at 80% power)'.

lessons.lesson: Registry data often distinguishes 'Not recommended' (provider) from 'Patient refused' (patient). When the disparity is concentrated in 'Not recommended' with identical 'Patient refused' rates, this is strong evidence for clinician-driven mechanism.
lessons.action: When studying treatment-receipt disparities, analyze the reason-for-no-treatment field by race. Provider-side excess + identical patient-side refusal = evidence for clinician-driven mechanism. Caveat that registrar-coded reasons are not audited clinical decisions.

lessons.originating_session: V4 Esophageal Cancer Disparity Analysis (cross-cohort harmonization)
lessons.lesson: Comparing two national cancer registries (SEER, NCDB) without harmonizing inclusion criteria conflates real disparity differences with cohort-definition differences. Histology coding, first/only-primary filtering, and year-window choice all matter.
lessons.action: Apply identical inclusion criteria to both cohorts before any cross-cohort comparison: (a) ICD-O-3 site, (b) histology codes, (c) first/only-primary filter, (d) year window, (e) age, (f) race-known. Document the harmonization cascade in a CONSORT-style supplementary table.

lessons.lesson: Internal hypothesis IDs (Q-numbers, internal labels) leak into manuscript tables if the table generator dumps raw analysis sheets. JAMA tables require lettered footnotes (a, b, c), No./total No. (%) format, exact P-value rules, single-spaced 10-12pt, table grid only.
lessons.action: When building manuscript tables: (1) replace internal IDs with descriptive endpoint labels; (2) use lettered footnotes (a, b, c) tied to specific cells/headers; (3) format P values as JAMA spec (.56, .003, <.001); (4) include numerator/denominator with %; (5) put SD/IQR inline with mean/median; (6) no merged cells; (7) one piece of data per cell.

lessons.lesson: JAMA Network journals require: 2 digits past decimal (.56); 3 digits if <.01 (.003); <.001 if smaller; never P=0 or P=1; never report P alone without effect size and 95% CI.
lessons.action: Use a standard P-value formatter that enforces all four rules. Apply to every reported P in tables, results text, and abstract. Always pair with effect size + 95% CI; never use star notation alone.

lessons.originating_session: BioMedAgent methodology adaptation (Bu et al, Nat Biomed Eng 2026)
lessons.lesson: Single-shot analysis or single-shot drafting fails for complex clinical-research tasks. The BioMedAgent insight applies: break the work into Plan → Execute → Verify with explicit handoffs and self-correcting feedback.
lessons.action: Apply three-phase pipeline: Phase 1 Plan (estimand, inclusion criteria, method, sensitivity analyses pre-specified); Phase 2 Execute (script every step, save to date-stamped folder); Phase 3 Verify (numerical, distributional, clinical sanity check). If verification fails, return to Phase 1, not Phase 2.

trigger_patterns: research question framed as natural language
lessons.lesson: BioMedAgent's task classification (omics / precision medicine / ML / stats / viz) maps to clinical-research as: descriptive / inferential test / multivariable / survival / sensitivity / subgroup. Method selection is downstream of classification.
lessons.action: Before choosing a method, classify the request into one of six categories using the routing table in references/biomedagent-methodology.md. A request that spans categories ('compare groups, adjusted for X, in patients with Y') should be planned as a sequence of steps across the relevant categories, not collapsed into one model.

lessons.lesson: BioMedAgent's Redis memory cache lets it skip planning steps it has solved before. Re-deriving a known pattern wastes time and risks introducing errors. The clinical-research equivalent is this lessons-log.json.
lessons.action: At the start of every session, scan trigger_patterns in this file for matches to the current task. If a match is found, apply the entry's action directly. Skip steps that have been solved before. After the session, append new entries for new patterns.

lessons.originating_session: Bilal Mirza abstract editorial philosophy (12-principle rubric)
lessons.lesson: Coherence is the primary editing criterion for an abstract. Every element must earn its place by serving a single mechanistic or narrative arc. If a finding can be removed without weakening the story, it should be moved to the manuscript.
lessons.action: Before submitting any abstract, ask: 'is this one story or two?' If reviewers will wonder why two ideas are in the same abstract, the framing — not the science — is the problem. Demote the secondary thread to manuscript supplementary; rewrite the objective so the surviving thread is clearly the focus. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 1.

lessons.lesson: Hypothesis-falsification narratives are publication-strong. A pre-registered candidate mechanism that fails, paired with an alternative mechanism that succeeds, is the Mariathasan 2018 architecture and is preferable to either a pure positive finding or a pure negative one.
lessons.action: Structure: prior literature anchors the candidate -> we tested it -> it failed -> here's what we found instead. Falsification language must appear in three places (objective, results, conclusions) so a fast-scanning reviewer cannot miss the arc. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 2.

lessons.lesson: Calibrated language tracks epistemic standing. Null results with N<100 do not 'refute' — they fail to support, provide no evidence for, do not predict. Cross-sectional associations do not 'predict treatment benefit' — they support a hypothesis-generating rationale for testing. Architecture findings in stratified analyses describe phenotypes enriched in sub-cohorts, not phenotypes characteristic of populations.
lessons.action: Flag-list of verbs/phrases that trigger re-examination: 'is refuted', 'is characterized by', 'independent of', 'predicts', 'drives', 'establishes'. They may be earned, but they often aren't. The fix is rarely deletion; usually a hedge, a cohort qualifier, or a verb downgrade. Add cohort qualifier ('in this cohort,' 'in the Asian sub-cohort') to preserve accuracy without losing impact. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 3.

trigger_patterns: TCGA / SEER / NCDB / GENIE cohort by race
lessons.lesson: Race terminology must match what was actually measured. Use the labels the source dataset used (TCGA -> Asian/White/Black; SEER/NCDB -> NHB/NHW/Hispanic/NHAPI). Do not promote self-reported race to genetic-ancestry vocabulary unless germline SNPs were measured.
lessons.action: Add 'self-reported race per registry coding' once in Methods to preempt the reviewer question (Carrot-Zhang 2020 framework). Avoid 'Caucasian' (deprecated), 'Oriental' (offensive), and ancestry abbreviations without ancestry data. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 4.

trigger_patterns: Methods section approaching size of Results
Conclusions summarizes results rather than interpreting them
lessons.lesson: Section weight signals priority. Results should be the largest section by character count — at least twice Methods and twice Conclusions. Methods compresses to the minimum that establishes rigor. Objective frames the question and prior hypothesis. Conclusions states what findings mean and what comes next, not a recap.
lessons.action: If Methods or Conclusions approaches the size of Results, the abstract is mis-weighted. Trim Methods to its rigor essentials (pre-registration, FDR control, key analytic move). Re-write Conclusions as interpretation, not recap. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 6.

trigger_patterns: draft says 'predicts preferential benefit from drug X'
cross-sectional transcriptomic data linked to therapy
lessons.lesson: Therapeutic implications must be claimed at the level the data supports. Cross-sectional transcriptomic correlations support trial-design rationale, not treatment-response prediction.
lessons.action: Use 'supports the rationale for testing X' or 'provides a hypothesis-generating rationale for evaluating X' — never 'predicts preferential benefit from X' unless a treatment-response analysis was performed. Standard test: would I be embarrassed at the podium if a discussant asked which patients in my dataset received this therapy? See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 7.

lessons.lesson: Honesty over impact when they conflict. If a softening edit costs perceived impact, take the softening. Editorial momentum tends to push abstracts toward over-claiming; the discipline is pushing back.
lessons.action: Flag-phrases for re-examination: 'is refuted', 'is characterized by', 'independent of', 'predicts', 'drives', 'establishes', 'demonstrates', 'proves'. The fix is rarely deletion; usually a hedge, a cohort qualifier, or a verb downgrade. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 9.

trigger_patterns: claim being elevated from supplementary to abstract
lessons.lesson: Iterate against the four-criterion gate before claims become abstract-grade: (a) BH-FDR q<0.05, (b) bootstrap BCa 95% CI excludes null, (c) permutation p<0.05, (d) jackknife sign-stable across leave-one-out resamples.
lessons.action: Findings that are directional or pass only nominal FDR (q<0.10) belong in supplementary, not the abstract conclusion. The therapeutic-implication sentence should be anchored on findings that cleared the gate, not on borderline results. See write-abstract/SKILL.md Principle 11.

lessons.lesson: Compliance is non-negotiable and checked last. Character count, four-section bolded headers, absolute numbers alongside percentages, no institutional or product names in body, no abbreviations outside accepted list without first-use spell-out, generic drug names — these are absolute. Confirm character count after every revision.
lessons.action: Before declaring an abstract submission-ready, run the 12-point gate explicitly (write-abstract/SKILL.md). Output a structured table noting Pass/Fail and suggested fix for each principle. Do not declare submission-ready until each row is either pass or has an explicit accepted exception. Most-impactful priorities: 3, 7, 9 for active editing; 1, 2, 6 for narrative architecture; 10 for compliance; 11 for rigor.

lessons.originating_session: Bilal Mirza standing rule — every analysis must end with a structured report
trigger_patterns: session ending with results but no report
manuscript Methods being drafted without an upstream report
future session re-deriving analyses already done
lessons.lesson: Every analysis must end with a date-stamped, structured markdown report. The report is the durable deliverable that future sessions, co-authors, and reviewers can audit. Without it the analysis is incomplete: there is no single source of truth for what was done, the manuscript Methods get re-derived from chat history, and reviewers cannot trace numbers to their generating script.
lessons.action: Generate a markdown report at the end of every analysis session. Filename pattern: analysis_report_<short-question-slug>_YYYY-MM-DD.md. Save to the project's Analyses/ or Reports/ folder. Required sections (in order, no omissions): header, research question, estimand, data sources, cohort selection cascade, variables, statistical methods, pre-specified sensitivity analyses, results (cohort characteristics + primary + secondary + sensitivity + subgroup + multiple-testing master), diagnostic checks, findings summary in plain language, limitations, reproducibility checklist, files referenced cross-walk, verification trail, next steps. Every claim includes effect size + 95% CI + P value + BH-FDR Q where applicable. Every percentage paired with numerator/denominator. Re-generate when the analysis is re-run; archive prior versions rather than overwriting. Full template lives in analyze/SKILL.md Step 9.

lessons.originating_session: HNSCC-TAM Multi-Cohort Validation (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona) — pre-submission rigor pass
trigger_patterns: any manuscript with >5 cohorts or >5 statistical claims
lessons.lesson: Before drafting a manuscript from already-completed analyses, run a structured 5-agent self-audit (numerical, statistical, biological-plausibility, code-reproducibility, completeness). The HNSCC-TAM session caught 4 critical issues — wrong AUROC value, pseudoreplication in cross-compartment correlations, an N/N validated overclaim, and missing random seeds — none of which were detectable from the report itself without comparing claimed numbers against source CSVs and re-deriving p-values at the proper sample size.
lessons.action: Whenever an analysis is being staged for manuscript drafting, run a 5-agent self-audit producing AUDIT_REPORT.md with severity-graded findings (CRITICAL / HIGH / MODERATE / MINOR). Each finding cites the source CSV row and the claimed value. Do not begin manuscript drafting until critical issues are resolved or explicitly deferred with a documented gating plan.

trigger_patterns: manuscript drafting blocked on rigor issues
user wants to systematically address audit findings
BCa CIs and BH-FDR not yet applied
lessons.lesson: A 6-phase remediation sequence cleanly closes most audit issues without requiring a full pipeline re-run: (1) surgical text/numerical fixes against source CSVs, (2) bootstrap BCa 95% CIs + BH-FDR + Bonferroni multiple-testing correction, (3) patient-level cross-compartment re-derivation + drop-LOO sensitivity, (4) canonical meta-validation table consolidating all evidence, (5) mandatory 16-section analysis report, (6) numerical re-audit cross-walking all claims against source CSVs.
lessons.action: Set up phase-numbered remediation folders (phase1_remediation/, phase2_remediation/, ..., phase6_audit/) under the project Analysis/ root. Each phase produces (a) one Python script in scripts/, (b) machine-readable outputs in results/, and (c) one PHASE*_REPORT.md narrative. Phase 6 must be a programmatic re-audit producing a PASS/FAIL log — target 100% PASS at 3-decimal precision before manuscript submission.

trigger_patterns: paired pre/post scRNA cohort with small n_patients
claim of n=8 with 4 patients × 2 timepoints
Spearman rho with degrees of freedom > true patient n
lessons.lesson: When a small discovery cohort (e.g., n=4 patients with paired pre/post sampling) is analyzed by treating each (patient, treatment) pair as an independent observation, the resulting n=8 correlations are pseudoreplicated and overstate statistical certainty. The proper patient-level analysis is n=4 (averaging pre+post within patient or using pre-only). At n=4 the minimum two-sided EXACT Spearman p-value is 2/24 = 0.083 — a structural floor, NOT a property of the data — so no patient-level n=4 correlation can reach p<0.05 by exact testing.
lessons.action: For any correlation in a small discovery cohort with paired timepoints, derive THREE framings: (a) pseudorep n_obs (sanity check vs original number), (b) patient-level n_patients (averaged or pre-only — addresses pseudoreplication), (c) pre-treatment baseline only. Use scipy.stats.permutation_test with permutation_type='pairings' for n<=5 (full enumeration) or n_resamples=10_000 for n=6-8 (Monte Carlo). The asymptotic Spearman p-value formula breaks at |rho|=1 (returns p=0 spuriously); always use exact / Monte Carlo for small n. Reframe the patient-level finding as HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING in the manuscript.

trigger_patterns: any subtype analysis where N cells >> N patients
lessons.lesson: In small scRNA-seq discovery cohorts, a single patient can dominate a cell-type pool. In the HNSCC-TAM session, Patient_2 contributed 96.66% of all TREM2+ TAM cells (984 of 1,018). Every TREM2+ TAM downstream finding (DEGs, TF activity, metabolic programs, ligand-receptor scores) is mathematically a Patient_2 finding. The dramatic 0.32x post-treatment fold change attenuated to 0.44x with Patient_2 dropped, and the Wilcoxon p-value remained non-significant in either framing.
lessons.action: For every cell-type / subtype analysis in a discovery cohort with n_patients <= 6, compute per-patient cell-pool contribution (n_cells_patient_X / n_cells_total). If any patient contributes >50% of any subtype, generate an automatic Drop-Patient-X leave-one-out sensitivity analysis covering: (a) per-patient mean proportion with vs without that patient, (b) Pre/Post fold change with vs without, (c) Wilcoxon paired test in each framing. Report both versions in the manuscript and reframe the finding as HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING if the magnitude is patient-dependent.

trigger_patterns: AUROC reported in clinical research manuscript
lessons.lesson: Reviewers of biomarker-prediction manuscripts routinely flag 'AUROC without 95% CI' as a critical methodological gap. Point estimates without uncertainty bands are not interpretable, especially at small N. Paired bootstrap with bias-correction-and-acceleration (BCa) 95% CI is the rigorous gold standard. At n=12 (TJU nivolumab stratum), some BCa CIs spanned [0, 1] — reflecting the true uncertainty.
lessons.action: Compute paired bootstrap BCa 95% CI on EVERY AUROC reported in the manuscript. Use 500-1000 paired (scores, labels) resamples with random_state=42; skip resamples that lose class diversity. BCa requires bias-correction z0 (from boot empirical distribution) and acceleration a (from jackknife). scipy.stats.bootstrap with method='BCa' and paired=True works; or implement manually with norm.ppf. Report as 'AUROC = X [95% BCa CI, low, high]' uniformly. Flag CIs that span 0.5 as 'not statistically distinguishable from chance.'

trigger_patterns: panel of N signatures tested in same cohort
any signature batch >= 5 in single cohort
lessons.lesson: When >=5 signatures are tested against the same outcome in the same cohort, family-wise testing inflates false positives. In GSE159067 (n=102, 14 signatures), three findings survived BH-FDR (CXCL9/10, CYT, CXCL9/CD8A) but only ONE survived Bonferroni (CXCL9/10 at alpha=0.05/14=0.0036). The Bonferroni-survivor is the load-bearing claim; BH-FDR-only survivors should be framed as 'supportive of, not independent confirmation of' the Bonferroni-survivor.
lessons.action: For every (cohort × signature panel) batch >= 5 tests, apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR using statsmodels.stats.multitest.multipletests(method='fdr_bh'). For the largest validation cohort (typically the manuscript's anchor), additionally apply Bonferroni at alpha = 0.05/n_tests. Report BH_q and Bonferroni_significant columns alongside raw P. Frame manuscript claims by tier: Bonferroni-survivor = lead finding; BH-FDR-only = supporting; raw-P-only = hypothesis-generating.

trigger_patterns: any stochastic call without explicit seed
lessons.lesson: Without random_state=42, scanpy's sc.tl.pca / umap / leiden / neighbors / diffmap calls produce non-deterministic results. Cluster identity may be preserved across runs (Leiden is deterministic given seeded igraph + identical input) but cluster *labels* permute, which can scramble downstream analyses.
lessons.action: Add a seeded helper `seed_all.py` to every project that imports os, random, numpy, scanpy, torch and sets seed=42 across all of them. At the top of every analysis script, call seed_everything(42) before any stochastic operation. ALSO pass random_state=42 explicitly to every: sc.tl.pca, sc.tl.umap, sc.tl.leiden, sc.tl.tsne, sc.tl.diffmap, sc.tl.dpt, sc.pp.neighbors, sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split, sklearn.cluster.* . Use np.random.default_rng(42) instead of np.random.* for new code. Document this in CLAUDE.md as a project-wide rule.

lessons.lesson: After audit-driven rigor remediation, the manuscript's Table 1 should be a CANONICAL meta-validation table consolidating: (a) v1-corrected effect sizes, (b) bootstrap 95% CIs, (c) raw P-values, (d) BH-FDR q-values, (e) Bonferroni status, (f) status grading (ROBUST / PARTIAL / NOVEL / HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING / EXTERNALLY VALIDATED), (g) per-finding source-CSV provenance.
lessons.action: Build a Phase 4 consolidation script `phase4_build_meta_validation_v2.py` that reads all upstream CSVs (phase1_corrections, phase2_aurocs, phase3_xc_correlations) and produces: meta_validation_v2_long.csv (one row per Finding × Cohort × Statistic with full provenance), meta_validation_v2_summary.csv (one row per Finding with audit-correct headline), and meta_validation_v2.xlsx (formatted workbook). Headline-selection priority: EXTERNALLY VALIDATED > HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING (audit-corrected) > PARTIAL > ROBUST > NOVEL > largest-N fallback.

trigger_patterns: deciding what goes in abstract vs body vs discussion
lessons.lesson: After rigor remediation, partition every finding into 4 tiers for placement in the manuscript: TIER 1 (ROBUST + Bonferroni-survivor) leads the abstract and primary results; TIER 2 (PARTIAL = BH-FDR-only) appears as supportive in the body; TIER 3 (NOVEL single-cohort + EXTERNALLY VALIDATED cross-compartment) appears in main text + future-work hooks; TIER 4 (HYPOTHESIS-GENERATING) appears in Discussion + Limitations only — NEVER in the abstract or primary tables.
lessons.action: When drafting the manuscript, apply tier-based placement: (1) Abstract leads with Tier 1 findings + effect sizes + 95% CIs; (2) Results sections present Tiers 1-3 in the body with explicit multiple-testing flags; (3) Discussion §Limitations explicitly addresses Tier 4 as 'directionally consistent but not statistically distinguishable from chance after multiple-testing correction at this sample size.' This pre-empts reviewer critique of overclaiming.

lessons.lesson: Manual claim-by-claim verification of a multi-cohort manuscript is error-prone. A programmatic re-audit script that cross-walks every numerical claim in the manuscript against its source CSV at 3-decimal precision is the durable verification artifact.
lessons.action: Build a Phase 6 script `phase6_numerical_reaudit.py` that adds named checks via add_check(name, claimed, observed_in_source, tolerance, unit) and writes phase6_audit_log.csv + phase6_audit_summary.txt. Cover at minimum: (a) claimed text values vs source CSV cells, (b) summary-table values vs upstream long-table values, (c) document-internal stale-string scans, (d) sample-size sums. Target 100% PASS before submission. Produce AUDIT_REPORT_v2.md as the closure document.

trigger_patterns: PIs need a single brief vs full report
lessons.lesson: Multi-cohort manuscript projects accumulate multiple narrative documents. A clear hierarchy prevents confusion: (a) `Reports/insights_report_v*.md` = working insights document with full numerical detail; (b) `Reports/analysis_report_<question-slug>_<date>.md` = mandatory 16-section technical analysis report (per working-rules.md); (c) `Reports/FINAL_manuscript_brief_<date>.md` = manuscript-ready narrative brief consolidating story arc + cohort contributions + tier-graded evidence (this is the document PIs read first); (d) `Manuscripts/manuscript_complete_<date>.docx` = the actual manuscript deliverable.
lessons.action: Whenever a manuscript project exits the rigor remediation phases, produce the FINAL manuscript brief BEFORE drafting the manuscript itself. The brief contains: §1 Executive summary, §2 Why this project exists, §3 What we did chronologically, §4 Cohort-by-cohort contribution, §5 Four-tier evidence base, §6 Audit story, §7 Manuscript story arc with 3 title options, §8 Suggested abstract, §9 Section-by-section content map, §10 Path to submission, §11 Document index, §12 One-sentence elevator pitch. PIs review the brief first to confirm direction, then the manuscript drafts against the brief.

lessons.originating_session: Esophageal-IO V4 — race × era interaction audit (Bilal Mirza, U Arizona)
trigger_patterns: audit sub-question contains 'race × X' or any A:B interaction
interpretation prose substitutes one outcome's interaction P for another
deciding whether to report formal interaction tests in main text vs supplement
manuscript has comparator paper in same subfield
lessons.lesson: Two intertwined disciplines that the V4 audit had to learn the hard way. (1) AUDIT TAGGING: every audit step that involves an A × B interaction must be tagged with both `outcome` and `dataset` fields, and the audit-report prose must repeat that tagging. A P value computed on one outcome (e.g., surgery receipt) must NEVER be interpreted in the audit report as if it spoke to a different outcome (e.g., overall survival), even when the conceptual interaction question is the same. Cross-check the audit's Phase-1 gap list against the manuscript abstract's headline claims one-by-one before declaring the audit complete. (2) COMPARATOR-ALIGNED REPORTING: when choosing whether to report a formal interaction test in the manuscript main text, the most defensible default is to match the comparator paper's reporting practice in the same subfield and same journal tier. For disparity research published in JCO / Lancet Oncology / JAMA Oncology, the field-standard pattern (e.g., Miller 2024 Lancet Oncol on VHA ICI effectiveness by race) is descriptive within-subgroup analyses without formal interaction P values. Match that pattern; run the formal interaction test internally as an audit sensitivity so it is in the back pocket if reviewers demand it; report it only in supplementary or a reviewer-response packet.
lessons.action: When auditing a multi-outcome manuscript, tag every interaction-test entry with `outcome` and `dataset` fields. Run the formal interaction test for every claim that depends on effect modification, even if you do not plan to report it in main text — the result is needed for reviewer-response readiness. Before deciding what to report in main text, identify the closest published comparator paper (same subfield, same journal tier) and check whether they reported formal interaction tests; align your reporting to their practice unless your abstract makes a stronger effect-modification claim than theirs. Document the decision and the comparator citation in the audit report so future sessions do not re-derive the analysis from scratch. For Esophageal-IO V4 specifically: comparator is Miller S et al, Lancet Oncol 2024 (PMID 39551068; DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00528-X) — descriptive within-race subgroup reporting only; V4 main-text matches that practice; formal HCA-4 / HCA-9 / HCA-10 interaction results retained internally in the audit report as supplementary sensitivity.

lessons.originating_session: V4 Esophageal-IO — Miller-2024 comparator gap closure (V4_22 P1-P5)
lessons.category: Method: causal-inference rigor for within-treatment-recipient subgroup comparisons
trigger_patterns: within-recipient HR being reported in a multi-stage access-disparity manuscript
comparing observational results to an equal-access (e.g., VHA) effectiveness study
manuscript reports unmatched HR within ICI / chemo / surgery recipients
reviewer asks whether the within-recipient disparity persists after balancing on access factors
lessons.lesson: When a multi-stage access-disparity manuscript reports a within-treatment-recipient hazard ratio (e.g., NHB vs NHW within ICI recipients), an unmatched within-recipient HR is not a sufficient anchor — measured access factors at the moment of treatment receipt (timing from diagnosis to treatment, prior multimodal therapy, comorbidity, facility type, insurance) differ between groups, and these differences confound the within-recipient effectiveness estimate. The within-recipient HR must therefore come from a 1:1 propensity-matched analysis on those access factors, with a complementary IPTW sensitivity (logistic vs flexible-ML PS), a propensity-weighted KM figure, a histology-stratified version, and an explicit time-to-treatment Cox sensitivity. The pentet of outputs collectively determines whether the residual within-recipient disparity supports an 'access barriers operate even within recipients' framing or an 'after balancing, outcomes equalize' framing — the manuscript-language decision flips on this analysis.
lessons.action: Whenever a draft reports a within-treatment-recipient HR (or unadjusted within-recipient comparison) for an effectiveness disparity claim: (1) build a 1:1 propensity-matched cohort on the full access-factor set including timing-to-treatment; (2) prespecify the matched-HR cutoff that flips manuscript framing (e.g., HR >= 1.20 with CI excluding null -> retain access-barriers framing; HR ~ 1.0 -> rewrite to equivalence-after-matching); (3) replicate using a flexible-ML PS (gradient-boosted-tree IPTW) as a misspecification sensitivity, with a 0.10-HR-unit delta threshold for declaring concordance; (4) generate IPTW-weighted KM curves; (5) repeat the matched analysis stratified by the dominant biological subtype (e.g., EAC vs ESCC) AND test the formal interaction on the OUTCOME of interest (per L038, tag both outcome and dataset); (6) refit the matched Cox with treatment-timing as an additional covariate and report the Δ-HR explicitly, since timing is a key candidate mediator. In V4 (NCDB Stage IV ICI 2018+, NHW vs NHB) this yielded matched HR 1.25 (95% CI 1.03-1.53; P=.03), Δ(GBT-IPTW vs logistic-IPTW)=0.02 HR units, and ΔHR after time-to-ICI adjustment = +0.03 (timing accounts for none of the residual gap). The unmatched HCA-10 HR of 1.27 was therefore replaced by the matched HR of 1.25 in the V4 Discussion, retaining the access-barriers framing. See Scripts/V4_22_within_ICI_PSM_and_GBT_sensitivity.py and Section 8.10 of analysis_report_methodological_audit_2026-05-06.md.

lessons.originating_session: V4 PSM caliper-sensitivity review (external reviewer note)
trigger_patterns: reviewer asks whether caliper is binding
lessons.lesson: When a PSM caliper-sensitivity table shows nearly identical matched-pair counts across several caliper widths (e.g., 0.10 / 0.20 / 0.25 × SD), the cause may be either (a) the caliper is not binding because PS overlap is excellent (a substantive robustness finding) or (b) a bug in the matching loop that fails to apply the caliper. The two are indistinguishable from the count alone; an extended diagnostic sweep is required.
lessons.action: For every PSM analysis, run an extended caliper sweep at 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00 × pooled SD logit-PS, plus an unbounded sweep (no caliper). For each caliper, report (1) matched-pair count, (2) % of treated successfully matched, (3) median actual match distance, (4) maximum actual match distance, and (5) explicit Yes/No flag for whether the caliper is binding (max distance ≈ caliper). If the caliper is not binding for conventional widths (≥ 0.05 × SD), state this explicitly in the manuscript as a substantive robustness finding rather than as a placeholder caveat. Diagnostic script template: V4_18b_caliper_diagnostic.py.
